additionalTime: 30 minutes short-rib soak plus 6 to 24 hours marinating
author: Robert Carr
description: Korean BBQ galbi with flanken-cut beef short ribs, pear-soy marinade, garlic, ginger, sesame, scallions, grilled onion, and a simmered glaze for charred edges.
equipment: Outdoor grill, cast-iron skillet, carbon-steel skillet, or grill pan
ingredients: 1 1/2 pounds flanken-cut beef short ribs, cross-cut through the bone 1/4 to 1/2 inch thick, usually 4 to 6 strips
1/2 medium Asian pear or Bosc pear, peeled and cored, using 1/3 cup finely grated pear and juice
1 small yellow onion, half finely grated for 3 tablespoons onion and juice and half sliced into 1/2-inch rings for grilling
4 garlic cloves, finely grated or minced
1 1/2 teaspoons peeled fresh ginger, finely grated
3 green onions, 2 thinly sliced for the marinade and 1 thinly sliced for garnish
1/3 cup low-sodium soy sauce
2 tablespoons packed light brown sugar
1 tablespoon honey
1 1/2 tablespoons mirin or rice wine
1 1/2 teaspoons rice vinegar
1 teaspoon gochujang
2 teaspoons toasted sesame oil, divided
1 tablespoon toasted sesame seeds, divided
3/4 teaspoon black pepper, freshly ground if possible
Small pinch Diamond Crystal kosher salt or Morton kosher salt, for the onion rings
2 teaspoons neutral high-heat oil, such as avocado, grapeseed, canola, or vegetable oil, for the grill or pan
3/4 cup uncooked short-grain white rice, cooked for serving
1 head red leaf lettuce, leaves separated, optional for wraps
8 ounces kimchi, optional for serving
instructions: Place the 1 1/2 pounds flanken-cut beef short ribs in a large bowl of cold water for 30 minutes, changing the water once after 15 minutes if it looks very cloudy.
Drain the 1 1/2 pounds short ribs, then rinse each strip briefly under cold running water to remove bone dust from the cross-cut bones.
Pat the rinsed 1 1/2 pounds short ribs very dry with paper towels and arrange them on a rimmed baking sheet.
Trim away only any thick loose knobs of surface fat from the short ribs, leaving the thin marbling and small fat edges attached for flavor and browning.
Finely grate enough peeled pear to measure 1/3 cup packed pear and juice.
Cut the yellow onion in half, finely grate one half until you have about 3 tablespoons onion and juice, and slice the remaining half into 1/2-inch rings for grilling.

In a mixing bowl, whisk together the 1/3 cup grated pear and juice, 3 tablespoons grated onion and juice, 1/3 cup low-sodium soy sauce, 2 tablespoons packed light brown sugar, 1 tablespoon honey, 1 1/2 tablespoons mirin or rice wine, 1 1/2 teaspoons rice vinegar, 1 teaspoon gochujang, 1 1/2 teaspoons toasted sesame oil, 4 finely grated garlic cloves, 1 1/2 teaspoons finely grated ginger, 3/4 teaspoon black pepper, 2 thinly sliced green onions, and 1 1/2 teaspoons toasted sesame seeds.
Before any raw beef touches the marinade, spoon 1/4 cup of the clean marinade into a small saucepan and stir in the remaining 1/2 teaspoon toasted sesame oil.

Bring the 1/4 cup reserved clean marinade to a simmer over medium heat for 2 to 3 minutes, stirring often, until it thickens slightly into a glossy glaze; remove it from the heat and save it for brushing the cooked galbi.
Add the dried short ribs to the mixing bowl with the remaining marinade.
Use clean hands or tongs to turn and massage the short ribs in the marinade for 1 to 2 minutes, making sure the pear, onion, garlic, and scallions coat both sides of every strip.
Press the short ribs down into the marinade, cover the bowl, and refrigerate for at least 6 hours and up to 24 hours. For the deepest flavor with a clean beef texture, aim for 12 to 18 hours.
Take the marinated short ribs out of the refrigerator 30 minutes before cooking so the chill comes off while the meat stays cool.

While the short ribs sit at room temperature, cook the 3/4 cup short-grain white rice if serving with rice, separate the leaves from the red leaf lettuce if using, and put the 8 ounces kimchi in a serving bowl if using.
Brush the sliced half onion rings with about 1/2 teaspoon neutral high-heat oil and season them with a small pinch Diamond Crystal kosher salt.
For an outdoor grill, heat the grill to medium-high, about 450 to 500 degrees F, then clean and oil the grates with about 1 teaspoon neutral high-heat oil.
For indoor cooking, heat a large cast-iron skillet, carbon-steel skillet, or grill pan over medium-high heat for 4 to 5 minutes, then add 1/2 teaspoon neutral high-heat oil and work in batches.
Lift the short ribs from the marinade, let excess marinade drip back into the bowl for 3 to 5 seconds, and lay only as many ribs on the grill or pan as will fit in a single uncrowded layer.
Cook the first side of the short ribs for 2 to 3 minutes, until the edges are browned, the meat releases easily, and dark grill marks or seared patches appear.

Flip the short ribs with tongs and cook the second side for 1 1/2 to 3 minutes, until the meat is sizzling, caramelized, and at least 145 degrees F in the thickest meaty section if checking with a thermometer.
Brush each cooked side lightly with the simmered reserved glaze during only the last 30 to 45 seconds of cooking so the honey and pear turn shiny without burning.

Transfer the cooked short ribs to a clean wire rack or platter and repeat with the remaining marinated short ribs, adding another 1 teaspoon neutral oil to the pan between indoor batches if the surface looks dry.
Grill or sear the oiled half onion rings for 2 to 3 minutes per side, until softened and browned at the edges.
Discard the raw marinade left in the large bowl after the short ribs are removed.
Brush the hot galbi with any remaining simmered reserved glaze, then sprinkle it with the remaining 1 1/2 teaspoons toasted sesame seeds and the remaining thinly sliced green onion.
Rest the galbi for 3 to 5 minutes so the glaze settles and the meat juices stop bubbling.
Serve the galbi immediately with the cooked short-grain rice, grilled onion rings, separated red leaf lettuce leaves, and kimchi if using.
notes: Ask for flanken-cut beef short ribs, also sold as LA galbi or cross-cut short ribs. They should be sliced across the bones about 1/4 to 1/2 inch thick, not English-cut short ribs.
The 30 minute cold-water soak and quick rinse help remove bone dust. Dry the ribs well afterward so the grill browns instead of steaming.
Asian pear adds sweetness and helps tenderize the beef. Bosc pear is a good substitute and is gentler than kiwi or pineapple.
Use low-sodium soy sauce. If using regular soy sauce, reduce the soy sauce to 1/2 cup and add 2 tablespoons water to keep the galbi from tasting too salty after marinating.
The reserved glaze must be taken out before raw beef touches the marinade. Do not brush cooked galbi with leftover raw marinade.
A 12 to 18 hour marinade gives the best balance of seasoning and texture. Going beyond 24 hours can make the surface too soft because of the pear.
Medium-high heat matters. Galbi should cook quickly enough to char the edges while the thin short ribs stay juicy.
Brush the glaze only at the end of cooking because the pear, honey, and brown sugar can burn if they sit over direct heat too long.
If cooking indoors, turn on the vent fan, open a window if needed, and cook in small batches so the ribs sear instead of steaming.
Cut between the bones with kitchen shears at the table or before serving if you want smaller pieces for rice bowls or lettuce wraps.
Leftovers keep for 3 days in the refrigerator. Reheat quickly in a hot skillet with 1 tablespoon water, just until warmed through.
title: Korean BBQ Galbi
totalTime: 55 minutes plus 30 minutes short-rib soak and 6 to 24 hours marinating
